[29:33] So when we talk about inflammation, we are measuring inflammation.
[29:37] We look at ways to see inflammation in the body.
[29:41] So one of the first and easiest is a white blood cell count.
[29:44] Look at your CBC.
[29:46] If your white count is greater than 6.5 to seven, they're inflamed.
[29:52] So our goal is to be less than 6.5 to seven.
[29:57] Um, remember your, um, less than and greater than right is, um,
[30:03] greater than left is less than. Um, some of you do the alligator thing,
[30:08] but, uh, what's on here is less than 6.5 and seven to seven is your goal.
[30:15] Anything higher than that would be considered inflammation.
[30:19] Um, and the CBC we get on everybody.
[30:22] So even if you are in full blown mainstream medicine currently,
[30:27] and you get a CBC that comes back, you could talk to your patient about a
[30:33] more functional way to look at their CBC and simply say, look,
[30:38] this looks like you might have some inflammation.
[30:40] Don't you want to draw some more labs?
[30:42] Don't you want to figure out where the inflammation is?
[30:45] Um, and then you could start drawing some of these other levels.
[30:50] If you're already in your own practice or in a functional practice,
[30:54] you're probably able to draw any of these you want.
[30:57] CRP HS is C reactive protein,
[31:00] high sensitivity, or you can do a cardiac version of the CRP.
[31:05] Either one of those are much better than a plain CRP.
[31:08] A plain CRP is either going to tell you it's higher than three or less
[31:12] than three. That's not really helpful.
[31:14] Cause our goal is to get that CRP less than one.
[31:18] And so if the answer you're going to get is less than three,
[31:23] that doesn't tell us anything. It might be 0.6. It might be 2.9.
[31:29] And so those are vastly different and one is very inflamed and one is not.
[31:33] And so we want to make sure we always get either the cardiac CRP or the
[31:38] high sensitivity, be consistent.
[31:40] Now you can order the same one all the time.
[31:43] That doesn't always mean the lab is going to run the same one you want
[31:46] every time, but that is a challenge.
[31:50] Sometimes your technicians may transcribe it wrong or they,
[31:54] they see the letter CRP.
[31:56] And so they see CRP and they order the first one they come to,
[31:59] and it's not the right one.
[32:01] Sometimes when that happens on my next lab order to that company,
[32:05] that patient, I'll add a little note that says,
[32:09] please double check CRP order.
[32:11] Last time it was the incorrect test or something like that.
[32:15] So that they can look at that a little closer.
[32:18] Uric acid is discussed fully in Dr. Perlmutter's book,
[32:22] Drop Acid.
[32:23] Ideally he believes, and I do too,
[32:26] that uric acid should be less than five.
[32:28] And so if they have a uric acid over five or six or seven,
[32:33] they have systemic inflammation.
[32:36] That does not always mean it's gout or kidney stones.
[32:40] It could just be systemic inflammation.
[32:43] And if you get Dr. Perlmutter's book,
[32:45] he explains that in a lot more detail.
[32:47] Ideal homocysteine is around five to eight.
[32:51] So seven is sort of our target.
[32:53] If it's above eight or nine,
[32:56] that's typically associated with either an MTHFR variant
[33:01] or inflammation, sometimes both.
[33:04] If you have not done so,

[33:37] If your ferritin is much higher than your iron
[33:46] in number, not in measurement,
[33:49] we're basically just comparing numbers.
[33:52] That is now an inflammation marker.
[33:54] Ferritin is an acute phase reactant.
[33:58] Ferritin will go up in the presence
[34:01] of a sudden onset stress, flu,
[34:05] strep, cellulitis, otitis, whatever the illness is,
[34:12] ferritin can jump sky high randomly,
[34:15] suddenly without any warning.
[34:18] Remember that ferritin is a clotting risk.
[34:24] High ferritin poses high clot risks
[34:28] because the ferritin molecule is a sticky kind of aggregate.
[34:33] It's not a nice smooth molecule.
[34:36] When most of us draw thyroid labs,
[34:39] we usually draw the thyroglobulin antibody
[34:42] and the TPO antibody.

[38:32] and the thymazole because I had to learn to manage that

[38:48] is much more specific and beneficial
[38:53] when combined with some of these other tests.
[38:56] And so I started ordering the IFA part
[39:00] or the IFA version of the ANA, not the plain ANA.
[39:05] Kind of like the CRP and the CRPHS.
[39:07] It's more sensitive.
[39:09] It's more specific.
[39:11] So that's the one I usually order.
[39:13] I never order an ANA reflex because many times
[39:18] the ANA will be negative and yet their other markers
[39:23] might still be positive.
[39:25] So you can have an ANA negative rheumatoid arthritis patient.
[39:30] You can have an ANA negative Sjogren's patient.
[39:34] So always order them individually and separately,
[39:38] whatever you suspect.
[39:40] The other thing is many times the ANA will pop positive
[39:43] for maybe complement three and the RH factor.
[39:47] Well, what do you do with that now?
[39:52] So it can muddy the water
[39:55] if you don't know what you're looking for.
[39:57] So try to sort out which markers you think you need the most.
[40:02] I don't normally order a said rate,
[40:04] but some rheumatologists still do.
[40:06] It's kind of an old school inflammation marker
[40:09] so you can certainly order a said rate.
[40:12] RH factor, anti-CCP are the two main things
[40:17] to consider ordering for your rheumatoid patients.
[40:23] If you suspect rheumatoid arthritis,
[40:25] those are the two big tests to consider.
[40:27] If you suspect lupus,
[40:30] then there's no point in ordering an RH factor
[40:33] unless you also suspect it could be rheumatoid
[40:35] based on her joint pain and her butterfly rash.
[40:39] Think about the symptoms of these conditions
[40:42] and order what's most appropriate for your patient.
[40:45] Compliments three and four are mostly associated with lupus.
[40:50] And then your Smith antibodies, anti-DSCNA
[40:54] are mostly associated with shogrens
[40:56] and or other mixed connective tissue diseases.
[41:00] So sort it out in your mind based on the symptoms
[41:05] the patient reports, look up symptoms if you have to,
[41:09] and then order the pieces and parts that you need
[41:12] to kind of identify what's going on.
[41:14] But there's no point in ordering all of these
[41:16] if you suspect lupus.
[41:18] You don't need to do that.
[41:19] If you suspect rheumatoid, you don't need all of these.
[41:22] So pick and choose the ones that are most beneficial.
[41:29] The ones we look at generally are general inflammation markers.
[41:33] They are about systemic head to toe inflammation,
[41:37] your white count, your CRP, uric acid, homocysteine, ferritin if it's high.
[41:42] If the reverse C3 is suddenly high after it's been good,
[41:47] then consider you've got some inflammation, new onset, something,
[41:52] new stress, new something going on and talk to your patient.
[41:57] Maybe it's around August and everybody just went back to school.
[42:01] And now your patient comes to see you with a reverse T3 of 23
[42:06] and it's been 12 for months.
[42:09] And then I would look at that and say,
[42:12] well, didn't you just go back to work?
[42:14] You're a teacher.
[42:16] You're around a lot of snotty-nosed kids.
[42:18] You're taking care of a lot of sick kids at school.
[42:21] It might be the school nurse that's come to see you.
[42:24] So were you sick or exposed to sickness around the time of your draw?
[42:29] Remember, the immune system is likely to be triggered
[42:32] within a few days of exposure, even though you may not have symptoms.
[42:36] So that might impact your reverse T3.
[42:42] Thyroid antibodies that are barely above range,
[42:45] like if the range is up to 10 and they're 12,
[42:49] don't diagnose them with Hashimoto's like that.
[42:53] That's probably just systemic inflammation.
[42:57] If their thyroid antibodies are in the hundreds and thousands,
[43:01] that's Hashimoto's.
[43:02] But if it's barely above range, usually less than a hundred ish,
[43:07] I don't call it Hashimoto's unless the patient has known values
[43:13] that were way high before and he's been working hard to get them down.
[43:18] Just don't label patients inappropriately if you don't have to.
[43:23] Where does all this inflammation come from?
[43:25] Mostly our processed foods.
[43:28] Excess sugar will drive insulin up and insulin will push inflammation up.
[43:35] All of our seed oils that were intended to be lubricants
[43:40] and then they tweaked them a little and said, oh, they're safe to consume.
[43:44] They're good for your heart.
[43:46] All of those are highly inflammatory.
[43:49] Your corn oil, vegetable oil, canola oil.
[43:52] Those are all unhealthy.
[43:55] Many people are now food sensitive, not necessarily not necessarily allergic,
[44:01] but we do a lot of stuff in America to our harvest.
[44:06] We process the heck out of foods.
[44:08] We strip it of all nutrition and then we throw a little B12 back in
[44:12] and a little folate back in it.
[44:13] We say it's enriched and it's healthy.
[44:16] It's not.
[44:17] All of that is very inflammatory.
[44:20] People are very sensitive to wheat, gluten, and grains in general.
[44:25] They are very sensitive to dairy in a lot of cases.
[44:29] You can get the A1, A2 protein thing sorted out with some of your patients
[44:34] if they have access to that.
[44:37] Some patients are able to use farm raised raw milk from the local dairy,
[44:46] the local farm, with minimal issues.
[44:49] But in general, especially for your autoimmune patients,
[44:53] getting rid of grains and dairy is really, really, really important.
[44:57] Many people are allergic to or sensitive to eggs and soy,
[45:03] so keep an eye on that.
[45:05] Most of our patients have some form of gut issue.
[45:08] SIBO and yeast overgrowth are very common.
[45:11] Yeast is like, it might as well be the common cold these days,
[45:16] like everybody has yeast overgrowth.
[45:18] And so that impairs protein absorption, nutrient absorption.
[45:22] Everybody's chronically stressed.
[45:24] They don't get good sleep.
[45:26] We've all been exposed to a boatload of environmental toxins.
[45:30] Mold milled you in our bathrooms and our homes,
[45:34] especially if we live in damp, moist areas, beachy areas.
[45:39] Those kind of places can impact the housing that's nearby.
[45:44] Plastics leach into foods when they're heated in the microwave
[45:48] or foods left sitting in them for long periods of time.
[45:52] So be careful with plastics.
[45:54] We've switched to all tumblers.
[45:59] This is a healthy kind of plastic.
[46:03] I do have some metal straws that I prefer and use most often.
[46:09] So there are ways to switch out from using all the plastics,
[46:14] so be careful with that.
[46:15] Everybody has insulin resistance.
[46:18] Depending on the statistics you read,
[46:20] eight or nine out of 10 people have insulin resistance.
[46:24] That's everybody.
[46:26] And then that is going to drive a lot of hormonal balance
[46:29] as well as the inflammation sort of around Robin.
[46:33] Inflammation will cause some hormone imbalance.
[46:35] The hormone imbalance will respond
[46:37] by contributing to more inflammation.
[46:40] And then, of course, chronic infections.
[46:42] Patients that have interstitial cystitis,
[46:45] that's an inflammatory condition.
[46:47] Often very responsive to hormone, estrogen use,
[46:51] so that can be helpful.
[46:53] And then there are sneaky, stealthy pathogens and germs
[46:56] that keep people chronically sub-clinically sick,
[47:00] especially things like COVID and parasites.
[47:03] So lots of ways that you can find inflammation.
[47:08] When you're looking at a white count,
[47:10] these are a bunch of the things that will drive white counts up.
[47:15] We're familiar with an acute otitis, an acute pneumonia,
[47:19] an acute strep infection.
[47:21] We know if we draw blood,
[47:23] we're going to see those elevated white counts on those patients.
[47:26] But we don't always look at those levels
[47:30] that are normal but at the top end of the range.
[47:35] And those are the ones that are going to be
[47:37] what I call subtle chronic inflammation
[47:41] that's just floating around and hanging out.
[47:44] But that's not healthy.
[47:46] Some patients say,
[47:47] well, I thought that's what your immune system's supposed to do.
[47:49] And it is.
[47:51] But when it does it long-term
[47:54] and on a day-to-day basis, month-to-month basis,
[47:58] it's kind of like you send in the National Guard out there
[48:02] to work 24-7.
[48:04] They never get a rest.
[48:06] They never get to go to sleep.
[48:07] They don't get a break.
[48:08] They don't get to go eat.
[48:10] What's going to happen to those guardsmen in that instance?
[48:14] They're going to wear out real quick.
[48:16] They're not going to function at the top of their game.
[48:18] And that's what happens to your immune system
[48:21] when those white counts are staying on the higher end
[48:24] or just above range.

[48:30] that are on this list that might raise white counts

[50:14] Graves is an autoimmune condition
[50:16] that results from not only genetic predispositions often
[50:22] but from poor immunity that we don't understand,
[50:28] that we don't know is poor.
[50:30] We don't see it.

[50:34] we're going to talk about tonight.
[50:42] He's 30 years old, she says.
[50:44] Okay.
[50:45] So when you're looking at a CRP HS,
[50:50] you want to consider cardiovascular risk.
[50:53] If you have a CRP HS that's running
[50:58] between about three and 10,
[51:01] that's almost guaranteed cardiovascular disease
[51:04] in most cases.
[51:07] That may be somebody you want to send
[51:09] for a coronary artery calcium scan.
[51:14] Look at their obesity and metabolic syndrome markers.
[51:18] Look at their A1C, their insulin level, hormone balance.
[51:24] What about chronic inflammatory diseases?
[51:26] Remember that autoimmune diseases run in packs.
[51:30] If you have a patient with type 1 diabetes,
[51:34] they are more likely to develop Hashimoto's
[51:37] or lupus or rheumatoid than any other body.
[51:40] So make sure you're aware of autoimmune diseases
[51:45] that run in packs.
[51:46] All of these can run in packs.
[51:48] And it's all related to the same thing.
[51:50] If we don't support a good immune system,
[51:53] then when genes are flipped on or off,
[51:56] when things happen genetically or environmentally,
[51:59] the body is unable to respond appropriately.
[52:02] And so that's what allows for more
[52:04] fulminant disease production.
[52:10] Uric acid conditions,
[52:13] like everybody thinks about gout and kidney disease,
[52:16] kidney stones, but we don't think about some of these others.
[52:19] So look at what's common in all of these inflammation markers.
[52:25] Metabolic syndrome, insulin resistance,
[52:27] all of these are associated with these inflammation markers.
[52:32] So key takeaways with your CRP
[52:35] is to recheck it about every nine to 18 months.
[52:39] I usually do it about once a year.
[52:41] There is never any reason to redraw
[52:44] a CRP sooner than a year-ish.
[52:49] Nine months is the absolute earliest.
[52:52] It is going to take a long time to turn off
[52:55] inflammatory processes.
[52:57] They might remove all gluten, all toxins,
[53:00] all inflammation the first three or four months
[53:03] that you see them.
[53:04] But that doesn't mean that the inflammation process
[53:07] has suddenly stopped too.
[53:09] Remember, physiologically, things are going on
[53:13] within the body that don't magically disappear
[53:17] just because the inflammation root cause disappeared.
[53:21] Your insulin doesn't magically go down
[53:23] just because you go carnivore.
[53:25] It takes a while to have the body respond
[53:29] to all these changes and processes.
[53:32] So don't get frustrated with not seeing your markers move
[53:37] when they weren't going to move.
[53:39] So just don't draw them.
[53:42] Tell your patients it's going to take a long, long time.
[53:44] It's going to take six or eight, 10 months
[53:46] for us to see much improvement in this marker.
[53:49] There's no point in drawing it again.
[53:51] It just gets expensive.
[53:53] And the more times you do it,
[53:55] the more frustrated you get as the provider.
[53:58] And then your patients get really frustrated
[54:00] because they're like,
[54:01] I'm doing all this crap.
[54:03] I'm working hard.
[54:04] I'm not eating anything off plan.
[54:07] And my markers aren't getting better.
[54:09] You're going to lose their trust.
[54:12] If you draw these levels and their markers aren't changing,
[54:16] they don't understand the physiology.
[54:18] So just be sure that they understand
[54:21] it's going to take a long time.
[54:23] And so we'll recheck it in a year-ish.
[54:26] Jeannie, what did you say the cardiovascular
[54:28] disease number was for CRP?
[54:32] Usually between three and 10.
[54:35] If you have a CRP of 40, 30, 20,
[54:39] that's probably not cardiovascular disease.
[54:42] It's going to be systemic inflammation.
[54:45] It's going to be grain intake, ferritin that's high,
[54:49] insulin that's high.
[54:52] Thank you.
[54:53] Maybe an acute illness,
[54:55] a strep, cellulitis, a flu, something like that.
[55:00] But it's generally not going to be your cardiovascular disease
[55:04] when it's screaming high like that.
[55:06] It's not going to be your cardiovascular disease.
[55:08] It's mostly 10-ish or lower.
[55:11] Elevated homocysteine, you'll see a lot of cardiac trouble.
[55:17] But what contributes to cardiac trouble,
[55:20] insulin resistance and inflammation?
[55:22] So like some of these are just dominoes.
[55:25] MTHFR is another problem.
[55:29] Those are your methylation defects.
[55:32] And so I just encourage you to treat everyone
[55:35] as if they have MTHFR problems.
[55:38] And that way you don't have to keep separate
[55:41] who you're treating which way.
[55:43] Everybody in my practice gets treated as if they're positive
[55:46] because that's the most harmful piece of managing some of them.
[55:53] And so I just treat everybody as if they have elevated homocysteine.
[55:58] Even if their homocysteine is normal,
[56:00] I still treat them with methylation, methylated vitamins,
[56:04] methylated folate so that things get better.
[56:09] Elevated homocysteine is one of the more common causes
[56:14] of recurrent pregnancy loss.
[56:16] And so keep that in mind for your infertile patients.
[56:19] If you have a patient that has an elevated homocysteine
[56:23] and they report two or three pregnancy losses,
[56:26] consider genetic counseling for them
[56:29] and or getting a MMA and or MTHFR genetic test.
[56:37] You can run it with normal labs.
[56:39] You just want to be careful how you code it
[56:43] if you're letting them use their insurance
[56:45] because a lot of times insurance does not want to pay
[56:48] for genetic testing.
[56:50] You can go through RUPA and it's cash pay
[56:52] and it's not very expensive, I don't think.
[56:55] But that is an option to consider.
[56:59] With elevated homocysteine, they might be low in B12,
[57:04] they might be low in folate, they might be low in B6.
[57:07] So any of your B deficiencies can show up related
[57:12] to that elevation in homocysteine.
[57:15] In your Medicare patients, homocysteine will not be covered
[57:20] if you are seeing Medicare patients
[57:22] and sending them to the lab.
[57:24] Medicare will not cover a homocysteine level
[57:27] unless you use a hypertension diagnosis.
[57:30] So if they don't have high blood pressure,
[57:32] you cannot order a homocysteine on those Medicare patients.
[57:36] So make sure you delete that from your order
[57:39] or the patient can refuse.
[57:41] And I always tell patients, when you get to the lab,
[57:44] if they tell you your test, something's going to cost you
[57:47] an exorbitant amount of money, refuse that test.
[57:50] Just tell them, no, I'm not going to get that test.
[57:52] I'm not paying for it.
[57:55] And so if they don't get something we need,
[57:57] then we'll figure out what to do after that.
[57:59] But I always give patients the autonomy and authority
[58:02] to refuse a test if it's too expensive.
[58:10] The first thing to think of when homocysteine is elevated,
[58:12] of course, is a B12 folate problem.
[58:15] Usually methylmalonic acid and homocysteine
[58:19] can help you understand that because if both are high,
[58:23] then that's probably a B12 deficiency
[58:25] and probably MTHFR deficit.
[58:28] So this little screen might be helpful later on down the road.
[58:33] As ferritin, ferritin as an inflammation marker
[58:39] rises easily and quickly in illness, trauma,
[58:42] in a car wreck, a bike accident, a broken bone,
[58:46] anything like that can shoot your ferritin level up
[58:49] really, really high.
[58:50] Any kind of surgery, all of those things
[58:54] are going to trigger an acute inflammatory response,
[58:59] and that can contribute to high ferritin.
[59:03] I've said this before, and I say it lots.
[59:06] Iron and ferritin numbers need to be similar
[59:11] to be optimal in the 80 to 100-ish range,
[59:14] but not the measurements.
[59:17] We're not comparing the measurements.
[59:20] Ferritin is measured in nanograms per meal,
[59:23] and iron is measured in micrograms per deciliter.
[59:26] So they're completely different measurements,
[59:29] but what I find is that the patients do best
[59:35] when the numbers are close together.
[59:41] When ferritin is about one and a half
[59:44] or two times higher than the iron number,
[59:47] I call that inflammation.
[59:49] This example, iron is 67, ferritin is 100.
[59:53] This is probably an inflammation response.
[59:56] It's not quite one and a half, but it's not far.
[60:00] It might just simply be a vitamin D deficiency.
[60:03] Remember, vitamin D is required for ferritin
[60:08] to move around appropriately
[60:10] and for hepcidin to be kept low.
[60:13] We don't want high hepcidin,
[60:15] and that happens a lot when ferritin is really, really low
[60:19] or really, really high.
[60:21] Hepcidin gets lots of iron out of balance,
[60:24] and so simply adding vitamin D with its cofactors
[60:29] and its fat can correct the iron and ferritin dysfunction.
[60:36] And always keep in mind a gut problem.
[60:39] If they are reporting any kind of reflux,
[60:41] heartburn, indigestion, bloating, nausea,
[60:44] vomiting, diarrhea, constipation,
[60:47] then consider that some sort of gut dysfunction
[60:51] and consider a workup and or protocol for that.

[64:03] the better absorption we see.

[64:13] vitamins with their coffee and tea.

[64:19] Makes sense. Thank you.
[64:21] Vitamin C is great at helping to move iron around as well.
[64:26] It reduces inflammation.
[64:27] It is going to take out the trash, so to speak.
[64:31] That's the way I explain it to patients.
[64:33] So if you take vitamin C once a day,
[64:36] it's used up in about an hour, hour and a half.
[64:38] It's gone.
[64:39] So what do you expect that vitamin C to do for you all day
[64:43] or to do for your patient all day?
[64:45] So if your patient has Hashimoto's,
[64:47] you want to try to get them taking as much vitamin C
[64:50] as they can tolerate without diarrhea, literally.
[64:53] Because the more vitamin C they can get in their system,
[64:56] the better those Hashimoto's markers are going to look.

[65:02] all their other micronutrients.

[65:34] 360 to one?

[65:45] against that, especially when it's gone in literally minutes.
[65:49] And so trying to get your patients to take enough vitamin C
[65:53] all through the day can be a challenge.
[65:56] I usually just tell people take it as many times as you can
[65:59] until you find loose stool and then take a little bit less
[66:02] than that the rest of the time.

[67:02] We talked about this a little bit earlier, whoops.
[67:05] Thyroid antibodies may or may not be indicative
[67:09] of guaranteed autoimmune disease.

[67:20] like don't just label a patient that rates their insurance.

[67:46] or both are elevated, even if it's mild,

[67:55] because that'll even help you diagnose.
[67:57] So like if their TPO,
[67:59] antiroglobulin antibodies are like in the 30 or 40 range
[68:03] and they're not terribly high, they're just barely abnormal.
[68:08] You could say maybe we caught this autoimmune disease
[68:12] real early, but the guidelines do recommend
[68:17] when you have these numbers elevated
[68:19] that we get a sonogram of your thyroid.
[68:21] A lot of times you'll find nodules and goiters.
[68:23] You'll find the problems.
[68:25] And so it's helpful to go ahead and send them for that.
[68:30] And then we talked about the TSI
[68:33] if they are hyperthyroid.
[68:38] When they're hyperthyroid, they're going to be hyperstimulated.
[68:42] They're going to have heart rates greater than 100,
[68:45] usually greater than 150.
[68:47] They're going to be nervous.
[68:48] They're going to be anxious.
[68:49] They're going to tremor.
[68:51] They literally are going to be bouncing off the walls.
[68:54] Their blood pressure will be sky high.
[68:56] So with these patients, this symptomatic,
[68:59] we do typically put them on a beta blocker.
[69:02] The standard has always been propranolol.
[69:05] I have a problem using propranolol.
[69:08] Most of my patients don't like it.
[69:10] It causes such fatigue
[69:12] and they have to take it multiple times a day.
[69:14] And that's just not conducive to today's lifestyles.
[69:18] I typically put them on carbatalol.
[69:21] And I start with 3.25
[69:23] and bump them up to about 12 or so,
[69:29] whatever it takes to get their symptoms under control.
[69:34] You can start them on methemazole.
[69:36] I generally start 10 or 15 milligrams
[69:39] of methemazole once a day.
[69:42] If that helps, but it's not enough,
[69:44] you could do it BID for sure.
[69:48] But if you're not comfortable with that,
[69:51] punt them to endo, that's not a problem.
[69:54] They will put them on the methemazole,
[69:56] but get them on a beta blocker ASAP
[69:59] because hyperstimulation can result in cardiac dysfunction
[70:04] and an MI.
[70:05] And so you don't want to be responsible for that.
[70:07] Get them on a beta blocker no matter what.
[70:09] And if you're not comfortable doing the methemazole,
[70:12] then send them to endo until you get more versed,
[70:15] well versed or more comfortable with that drug.

[74:06] Yeah, but you can do that.
[74:10] When we talk about reducing inflammation,
[74:12] food is your primary method to reduce inflammation.
[74:16] Get rid of seed oils, vegetable oil, canola oil, corn oil.
[74:20] You can use avocado oil, olive oil.
[74:23] Don't cook with olive oil at high temperatures.
[74:28] Olive oil has been the mainstay of healthy oils
[74:33] and fats for decades,
[74:35] even in the midst of the old low-fat mantra.
[74:40] However, high temperatures of cooking with olive oil
[74:47] will contribute to the formation
[74:50] of polyunsaturated fatty acids, PUFAs,
[74:53] and those are inflammatory.
[74:55] So what I typically recommend is cook with your butter,
[74:59] cook with your lard, your tallow, your avocado oil,
[75:02] cook with any of your other oils,
[75:05] and if you want the flavor of olive oil,
[75:08] add it right before you serve it with the heat off
[75:10] so you're not running the temperature of your food
[75:13] really, really high,
[75:15] creating those polyunsaturated fatty acids
[75:18] that are inflammatory and dangerous.
[75:20] We don't want those.
[75:23] So think about all the ways that grains impact our health.
[75:29] It's not just about the grain.
[75:31] I think it was last week or a week before
[75:33] where we talked about, well,
[75:35] what if we just use ancient grains
[75:37] or we order them from France or England?
[75:40] Well, it's not just the grain.
[75:43] It's a carrier for other toxins used,
[75:46] whether it was planted, harvested, or processed.
[75:49] Even in your ancient stuff,
[75:51] it's still going to raise glucose,
[75:53] still going to raise insulin,
[75:55] it's still going to block nutrient absorption.
[75:57] So even if you use a less toxic form,
[76:02] it's not going to make all the problems go away.
[76:06] There are a lot of inflammatory diet conversations out there.
[76:12] AIP is a great way to get started
[76:15] when patients have autoimmune disease
[76:17] because it's right up their alley.
[76:19] It's an autoimmune protocol.
[76:22] And so you diagnose somebody with Hashimoto's,
[76:25] tell them, we need to be on this AIP diet.
[76:28] It's going to be grain-free.
[76:30] You don't have to tell them what it is all about up front.
[76:33] You can say, we're going to use this AIP diet.
[76:36] I want you to look this up on this website
[76:38] or listen to the speaker who talks about the AIP diet
[76:42] and how helpful it is.
[76:44] You have them working towards that inflammation reduction.
[76:48] You can use Whole30.
[76:50] Just remember that Whole30 is not very low carb.
[76:53] That was my problem with it.
[76:55] When I used it, I did not eliminate all the fruits.
[76:59] I thought those were free-for-all.
[77:02] So it didn't help my glucose and my insulin,
[77:05] but it was a great detox.
[77:08] So as I worked through, I did four months of Whole30.
[77:12] And every month, I tweaked more and tweaked more and tweaked more.
[77:16] And so as I learned what my body was doing
[77:20] and how I was responding,
[77:22] I did some more tweaking and wound up way lower fruit,
[77:25] way lower carb.
[77:27] Paleo is a great way to start.
[77:29] So there are bunches of options.
[77:31] I'm not a huge fan of Mediterranean diet,
[77:34] but if that is where you can get someone started, okay.
[77:37] Start there and then tweak that
[77:39] and reduce grains and fats in that later,
[77:42] or carbs in that later on.
[77:44] Stress is crucial.
[77:45] I cannot tell y'all how many patients I have
[77:48] that have high inflammation markers
[77:50] simply because of their stress levels.
[77:52] They get rid of the grains.
[77:53] They get rid of the inflammation.
[77:55] They get rid of everything except stress.
[77:58] And that just won't let anything get better.
[78:02] So make sure you address stress and sleep.
[78:07] We do have a whole session we're going to do on gut health
[78:11] in two or three weeks.
[78:13] And so we'll talk more about gut health,
[78:15] but I typically encourage patients
[78:18] to do a complete gut emptying.
[78:20] My personal preference of laxative is milk and magnesia.
[78:24] It's cheap.
[78:25] It's not very chemically.
[78:28] It's just milk and mag.
[78:31] You can use high-dose senna,
[78:33] but a lot of times that's not as effective.
[78:36] You can use high doses of salt in water.
[78:39] Make a soleil drink.
[78:41] You can use coconut oil.
[78:44] A tablespoon or two is a great laxative.
[78:47] You can put coffee and coconut oil together
[78:50] or even add your butter.
[78:52] You can use high doses of vitamin C every 15 minutes
[78:55] until you have very loose stool and empty everything out.
[78:59] So, like, there are bunches of ways to get the colon empty.
[79:03] If you have a patient going through a colonoscopy,
[79:07] make sure you help them address recolonizing the gut
[79:11] after that gut insult.
[79:12] That's a terrible gut insult
[79:14] and drives a lot of gut dysfunction.
[79:16] You think, oh, well, it's emptying everything out.
[79:18] It's great.
[79:19] Yeah, but as they put the unhealthy things back in,
[79:22] it just goes back to old patterns
[79:24] and the muscles go back to old behaviors.
[79:28] And so helping patients understand what to put in,
[79:32] how to recolonize the gut with healthy bacteria
[79:36] can be really helpful.
[79:39] One of your biggest problems with your IBS patients
[79:43] and your Crohn's patients
[79:45] are the terrible pieces of advice out there
[79:48] that mainstream medicine has been using
[79:50] to treat them for years.
[79:51] They tell them to add fiber, add grains,
[79:54] add vegetables, add broccoli, add corn,
[79:57] all the things that are irritants and roughage.
[80:00] Imagine putting that kind of mixture inside your mouth
[80:07] and just washing it around like a bottle brush.
[80:10] Take a bottle brush and stick it in your mouth
[80:12] and just scrub, scrub, scrub, scrub, scrub.
[80:14] That's exactly what these foods do
[80:17] to the mucous membrane lining of your intestines.
[80:21] Now, do you really want to do that to your intestines?
[80:24] No.
[80:25] We don't need that kind of cleaning.
[80:29] The gut is not designed for that.
[80:31] Our guts weren't really intended to digest all of this stuff.
[80:35] If it were, we could break down the roughage on the fiber.
[80:39] We can't break it down.
[80:41] So it goes through completely unchanged.
[80:44] And so that's not what digestion is about.
[80:47] So keep that in mind.

[90:34] Your levels won't be much different and your patients will be very frustrated.

[92:14] How do you decide to use it?
[92:16] Well, you have to partner with the patient.
[92:18] The patient's not buying in like the patient we were talking,
[92:21] I think it was Macy we were talking about earlier.
[92:23] Her patient didn't give up grains.
[92:25] She went from a ferritin of six to 11.

[92:48] and I know their problem is mostly inflammation,

[92:58] You should really consider this,
[93:00] but we can't really start you on it until you're grain-free
[93:04] because it just won't work.
[93:15] It can help with grains.
[93:17] It can.
[93:19] So the reason grains and low dose naltrexone don't work
[93:25] is think about the half-life.
[93:27] Low dose naltrexone has a half-life of about six hours, seven hours.
[93:32] By dose LDN, because it's going to help with rest and sleep,
[93:36] it's a downer in most people.
[93:39] So I'm going to dose it at suppertime bedtime.
[93:43] By the time they wake up in the morning
[93:45] and have their bowl of oatmeal,
[93:47] their oatmeal is going to drive inflammation back up.
[93:51] We dose their LDN at bedtime.
[93:53] It drives inflammation down overnight.
[93:55] They eat their oatmeal or their avocado toast
[93:59] and inflammation goes right back up.
[94:01] It's nothing but a fluctuation up and down rollercoaster
[94:05] of inflammation versus not.
[94:08] So I don't write LDN until they're grain-free
[94:13] or until they say they are.
[94:15] I don't tell them I won't.
[94:17] I just, you know, I talk to them and ask them,
[94:19] and I remind them that it's not going to be helpful.
[94:22] Insurance does not cover low dose naltrexone.
[94:25] I don't write PAs for low dose naltrexone.
[94:29] It's not that terribly expensive.
[94:31] I typically prescribe it in six week to 90 days intervals.
[94:37] And so this is how I write it.
[94:41] I start my first prescription of LDN at .5 milligrams
[94:46] and per Dr. Isabella Wentz's recommendations,
[94:49] I increase that dose every two weeks.
[94:53] If you increase LDN too much too fast or start too much,
[94:58] remember LDN is going to create problems similar to opioids.
[95:10] LDN works on the Mu and Kappa receptors.
[95:16] And so it's really important that we understand
[95:20] that opioids and LDN are going for the same receptor.
[95:26] So if they're on chronic pain management,
[95:29] LDN is not a great choice unless they're willing to get off.
[95:34] Maybe they've been weaning off of their opioids
[95:37] and they want to get off of it.
[95:46] LDN is compounded.
[95:49] It is not available at commercial pharmacies.
[95:51] There are some prescribers that will write
[95:54] a normal dose of Naltrexone and send it home
